summary: I was born in Honolulu, Hawaii in 1975. Growing up there, I experienced a unique blend of cultures that really shaped my identity. Honolulu felt like a place where many different people came together, which I think reflects the larger Hawaiian culture. It wasn’t until I moved away from Honolulu that I started to notice experiences like racism more distinctly.

Being born in Hawaii, I feel connected to its diverse cultural heritage. Hawaii is known for its great mixture of people, and that really influenced how I see myself and my background. I identify as a mix of Puerto Rican, German, Irish, and Cheyenne Indian, but living in Hawaii made me feel part of a community where such diversity is normal. It was only after moving to Washington that I encountered more overt racism, which was a stark contrast to my experience in Hawaii.

I consider myself a Democrat, although I wasn’t very politically active until about four or five years ago. Recently, I’ve been paying more attention to politics, studying pamphlets carefully before voting instead of just picking a name randomly. My involvement with the Democratic Party grew as I became more engaged with issues affecting gig workers, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic. This political awakening was part of my broader effort to advocate for better rights and benefits for workers like me.

I have been working for Grubhub for about four years as a gig worker. Grubhub allows me to work around my back pain because I can control my schedule and workload. I prefer Grubhub over other platforms because it offers more stable pay, especially compared to Postmates, which cut my pay drastically. Grubhub has been my main source of income since I transitioned fully into gig work after my back injury.

I sometimes work for DoorDash to keep my options open and stay current in the gig economy. I don’t rely on DoorDash as much as Grubhub, but I like having it as a fallback in case anything changes with my main platform. DoorDash offers me some flexibility, which is important given my unpredictable pain levels. I’ve been juggling DoorDash along with Grubhub for a few years now.

I used to work for Postmates, but I stopped because they changed their pay structure and cut my earnings in half overnight. That pay cut made it impossible for me to continue with them, so I shifted my focus to Grubhub and DoorDash instead. Postmates was part of my early gig work experience, but the sudden pay change was a big factor in leaving that platform.

I started getting involved with Working Washington at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic. Before that, I wasn’t very active, but when the pandemic hit, I realized things had to change for gig workers like me. Emily Dhatt from Working Washington reached out to me multiple times, encouraging me to speak at meetings and get involved. Through this organization, I became more politically engaged and hopeful that by banding together, gig workers could push for better pay, benefits, and rights.

The COVID-19 pandemic was a turning point for me both personally and professionally. During the pandemic, restaurants closed and many people turned to gig work, which changed the dynamics of my job. I started working earlier and longer hours, but there were fewer orders and longer wait times. The pandemic also motivated me to get involved with Working Washington to fight for gig workers’ rights, benefits, and fair treatment. It was a challenging time with shortages and new delivery protocols, but it also sparked my political activism.
